item.title: Fuckin bottom shelf
item.description:
I won't only be "30" ...but "in my 30s" Oh god
They got me last year...
@HileyNoteson Yoooooooooooooooooooooo
You can catch me live on twitch.tv/bottomshelfvodka streaming with my favorite Imgurians from the Bottom Shelf Community server.
Join for the games! We've been playing Lethal Company, Red Dead Redemption 2, Pay Day 3, GTA V, Jackbox, Among Us, 7 Days to Die, Sea of Thieves, Barotrauma, Minecraft, Ultimate Chicken Horse...if there's a specific game you like, I guarantee you'll find a shitposting meme loving Imgurian there to play it with you. Join for the memes! Imgurians share memes in the meme dumpster 24/7 Join for the Awe! Pay your pet taxes in the pet tex channel Join for the NSFW memes! (Thigh highs are still welcome) Join to chat with hilarious people! Join to lurk in the corners! Join for the shockingly magnificent balance of wholesomeness and chaos. discord.gg/bottomshelfvodka
Deaf cat Magoo (short for Oogala Magoogala) tax
item.dc:creator: BottomShelfVodka

item.title: Now that's what I call a sticky situation
item.dc:creator: kickarr

item.title: An island of meaning in an ocean of 400 billion suns
item.description: Professor Bran Cox at "A Symphonic Odyssey" with the Australian Sydney Symphony Orchestra describing what he said to World leaders at COP 26. "As far as we can tell, this planet is the only island of meaning in an ocean of 400 billion suns. Therefore if we destroy this then we may eliminate meaning in a galaxy of 400 billion stars, potentially forever: Discuss."
item.dc:creator: Malinut

Blame Imgur, because Weird Al is a national treasure.
item.dc:creator: HorrorMango

item.title: Choices
item.description:
I present to you two histories of men who were merely people like you and I, and the choices they made as they became Prisoners #1 and #2 of Auschwitz Concentration Camp. THE FIRST: Bruno Brodniewicz was born in 1895 in Poznan, then part of Germany. He was classified as a "habitual" criminal upon arrest in 1934 by the new Nazi regime and imprisoned in Lichtenburg concentration camp (at this point, still very much a traditional 'prison', housed inside a castle). He was later transferred to Sachsenhausen concentration camp sometime between 1934-1940. On 20 May 1940, he and 29 other prisoners from Sachsenhausen were taken by SS junior officer Gerhard Palitzsch to the newly-opened Auschwitz concentration camp (also called Auschwitz 1, this was followed the much larger Auschwitz 2 Birkenau, later to be followed by Auschwitz 3 Monowitz, attached to the nearby IG Farben complex). Brodniewicz and the other 29 "green" criminals (so-called as they wore green triangles, indicating they were criminals rather than political, religious, or ethnic prisoners) were selected by Palitzsch to take leadership positions over incoming groups of new prisoners as what became known as "Kapos", prisoners invested with small luxuries (such as private rooms) and extensive authority over others.
[Picture: September 1939, a German reconnaissance unit (probably part of General Wilhelm List's (1880-1971) 14th Army) takes a rest and refit in an then-unremarkable Polish town called Auschwitz (Oświęcim). Pasted in caption reads: "On the market square of Auschwitz, where we spent the night from September 3rd to 4th [the first week of the invasion]. Schütze Dominikus and Gefreiter Eggl with a real Kaftan Jew, whom we gave food. The area is teeming with such Jews. Their appearance is not exactly appetizing, but they are harmless, friendly and good-natured." Within days of this photo being taken, Jews were to be murdered in this square.] Gerhard Palitzsch (25 years old in 1940) was a man of exceptional sadism even amongst the SS, so it seems likely that he had deliberate intent in selecting Brodniewicz and other criminals for this role, wanting these criminals to act as a further layer of prisoner oppression below that of the SS and above the overall conditions of the camp. He may have been inspired by the Soviet Union's gulag system, where criminal prisoners were generally given free rein to terrorise political prisoners. Using prisoners as the 'day-to-day' oppressors of other prisoners was also a way of detaching SS troops from the mental stress of being daily inhuman - before the murder-camp system was inaugurated formally, "Einsatzgruppen" (Special Teams) - roving murder squads - were noted as suffering higher rates of alcoholism and mental distress from conducting mass murder after mass murder, usually by shooting, a comparatively intimate method of killing. Such intimate violence did not bother officers like Palitzsch, who is believed responsible for over 25,000 individual "back of the head" gunshot executions at the Auschwitz 'Death Wall'; including the execution of a family of five witnessed by survivor Boleslaw Zbozien (1909-1999, inmate number 22557), in which Palitzsch shot the children from youngest - a baby - to oldest, then the parents. Palitzsch was eventually dismissed from the SS for "racial defilement" - rape of Jewish prisoners - and is believed to have died while serving in a Wehrmacht Penal Unit in the Battle of Budapest in 1944.
[Picture: SS Sergeant Major Gerhard Palitzsch. An ironic victim of the racist ideals he served with utter brutality.] Bruno Brodniewicz took to this hideously cynical role - freeing up Nazis from the 'stress' of daily foregoing their humanity and handing the job to selected victims - with relish. He is believed responsible for the murder of an unknown amount of prisoners, but potentially in the hundreds. As "camp elder" (Lagerältester) his decisions on how he would 'work' the inmates under his control decisively set the atmosphere of most inter-prisoner relations in Auschwitz; creating a prisoner culture of exploitation, brutality, and manipulation, with green Kapos like Brodniewicz exploiting ethnic and political divisions amongst prisoners for their own benefit while maintaining good relations with the SS via continuous exemplary brutality on their charges. He was a 'victim' of this atmosphere himself when another green Kapo reported Brodniewicz's small horde of gold and valuables he had shaked down from inmates; Brodniewicz was briefly sent to Block 11 ("the punishment bunker", in which the first use of Zyklon B at Auschwitz was to take place), but subsequently Brodniewicz was circulated amongst a large number of Auschwitz subcamps, each time as "camp elder", in what appears to have been a deliberate effort by the SS to use him as a means of ensuring prisoner solidarity was undermined, suggesting his methods had became well-known, and appreciated, by the Nazis.
[Picture: a former Kapo is apprehended by Jewish community Police at the Zeilsheim (Frankfurt) displaced persons' camp in about 1946.] Brodniewicz, now nicknamed "the Black Death", was, in order, made camp elder in the Romani camp in December 1942, in June 1943 in Jaworzno sub-camp, from April 1944 in sub-camp Eintrachthütte and from September 1944 in sub-camp Bismarckhütte. After Auschwitz was partially evacuated in January 1945 as the Red Army closed in, Brodniewicz was transferred to the Woffleben subcamp of Dora concentration camp. Finally, sometime between January and April 1945, he was moved to Bergen-Belsen concentration camp. On April 15 1945 that camp was liberated by British forces. Brodniewicz was lynched along with 200 other Kapos by the now-free prisoners. Today "Kapo" is an incredibly virulent intra-Jewish slur, particularly in Israel. There are some indications in survivor testimonies that some Kapos attempted in small ways to help their charges, or create group solidarity (such as encouraging exemplary production) for the benefit of prisoner-overseer and overseen as a whole, but this was the exception rather than the rule; with such Kapos often losing their privileged position for being seen as 'soft' by the SS, in what again appears to have been a deliberate Nazi policy of ensuring prisoners turned on one another. One such exception was...
THE SECOND: Otto Küsel (born 16 May 1909), Auschwitz inmate number 2, was a burglar from Berlin. Like Brodniewicz, Otto Küsel was made an overseer, but also served as the administrator of the inmate Arbeitsdienst (labour service), so he would decide on the type of work assigned to prisoners. Küsel appears to have initially judged Auschwitz as "just another prison" and regarded the SS in the same way as prison officers - the natural enemy of the prisoner - identifying himself with other prisoners without regard for if they were political or ethnic prisoners supposedly 'below' him as an - albeit criminal - German. Not long after arriving at Auschwitz, however, he became an active anti-Nazi. He would assign exhausted prisoners to less demanding teams, and would assign prisoners active in the various attempts to plot resistance against the Nazis into teams which had long periods of relatively private interaction, such as those working the kitchens, hospital, or potato storage units.
[Picture: Otto Küsel after the war. In Auschwitz, he "bubbled with good humour and had a gift for putting everyone around him in a good mood." Notably, at one point he took despairing prisoners on to the roof of a barracks block, pointed to the countryside beyond the wire, and told them this day would pass, and they'd walk free one day.] In the memoirs of a large number of political prisoners, including resistance leader Captain Witold Pilecki (1901-1948, he famously deliberately entered Auschwitz to provide leadership to the Polish resistance in the camp) Küsel is unambiguously described as a saviour. He would also ensure that prisoners who were prepared to attempt escape were assigned to work groups who went outside the camp perimeter. As an overseer perk Küsel was permitted a violin by the SS, and he taught himself to play the Polish national anthem "Poland Is Not Yet Lost" (1797), which begins "Poland has not yet perished as long as we live" - a reminder of Poland's long periods as a nation without a country, which he would play to - and sing along with - prisoners while they worked. It was Küsel who reported Brodniewicz's horde of stolen valuables, and briefly freed Auschwitz from that Kapo's baleful presence. Prisoners who testified to his humanity & kindness include Józef Błasiński, Piotr Sadowski, Kazimierz Rawicz, Ryszard Dacko, Artur Krzetuski, Jerzy Pozimski, Stanisław Białas & Erwin Olszówka. Several prisoners, such as Eugeniusz Niedojadło, were penpals with Küsel after the war.
[Picture: Polish civilians of Oświęcim (Auschwitz), dig a defensive position (possibly a site for an anti-aircraft gun) in August 1939, shortly before the Nazi invasion. Pre-war Oświęcim's population was 13,000, of whom 9000 were Jewish.] In the summer of 1942, Küsel personally witnessed the murder of a German woman and her child by SS officer Hermann Kirschner (executed, 1948), after forcing the pair to undress and stand against a wall, they were shot, child first, with a rifle from relatively short range. In post-war testimony, Küsel reported that camp rumour was the woman & child were blameless of any crime, and was imprisoned because her husband was a Wehrmacht officer who had defected to British forces. This murder was immediately followed by that of another woman, who Kirschner killed with a point-blank pistol shot after the rifle he had just used to kill the child jammed. Küsel also briefly witnessed medical experimentation, when the boards covering the windows of a block put aside for that purpose warped and cracked in summer heat. He was in Auschwitz when the first purpose built Crematoria, Number 1, to burn the bodies of people executed, was installed by German Company Topf and Sons.
[Picture: the former HQ of Topf & Sons, the civilian company which was the largest provider of crematoria "ovens" to concentration camps. Today a museum of German civilian involvement in the Holocaust. The slogan is company motto "Always happy to be at your service", which was printed on the company stationary with which orders for mass people-burning systems were discussed with the SS. The company was nationalised by East Germany post-war, so survived until 1996; in 2001 the now-vacant site was occupied by activists to draw attention to the company's history, and it was turned into a museum in 2011.] He escaped from Auschwitz with three Polish prisoners on 29 December 1942. This was one of the most significant of the relatively small number of escapes from Auschwitz, being the first organised by the Polish resistance within the camp itself, but which nonetheless required collaborators in the Polish population outside the camp - many from the local area, such as the village of Oświęcim (Auschwitz in German, from which the complex of camps is named) were employed in construction and secondary services for the camp, such as laundry for the SS. Auschwitz was a formidable challenge to any escape attempt, of the over 1 million 300 thousand people who entered Auschwitz, a majority of whom were murdered shortly after arrival, only an estimated 928 attempted escape (0.007%) and of these only 196 (0.001%) were not killed or recaptured, living free but hidden until the end of the war. The most significant act of resistance at Auschwitz was a prematurely-launched uprising by Sonderkommando IV (a prisoner work group who operated a gas chamber, who had been stockpiling weapons and explosives from munitions works) in October 1944, which killed 3 SS Guards. Approximately 451 people were executed for this revolt.
[Picture: December 29 1942, Küsel and those he escaped with pictured in a Polish resistance safehouse in Broszkowice, only 3 miles from Auschwitz. L-t-R Mieczycslaw Januzewski, Jan Komski (1915-2002, number 152,884, post-war he worked for the US Newspaper the Washington Post), Otto Küsel, Boleslaw Kuczbara, with Mr and Mrs Harat, whose home it was. The second floor of this house had been converted into an apartment by the Harats, who rented it to a SS officer. It is unclear if the officer was in when this photograph was taken, but taking the photo at all was an act of extreme bravery.] Recaptured in Warsaw, he was returned to Auschwitz in September 1943 as a normal prisoner, and outraged his SS Guards by pretending to be Polish and not being able to speak German, leading to many beatings, including by SS-Untersturmführer Maximilian Grabner (1905- executed 1948), head of the Camp Political Department and counter-resistance efforts. He was kept in the prison block 11 in near complete darkness for several months, during which he met Józef Cyrankiewicz (number 62,933, 1911-1989) - future two times premier of Communist Poland (1947-52 & 1954-70).
[Audio: Küsel, as a witness in the Auschwitz trial of 1965, is asked by the judge if he was beaten by Maximilian Grabner during his interrogation after being re-captured. Küsel laughs at the question, "Yes (laugh) of course. I'd already been beaten!". Audio via auschwitz-prozess.de, from the Fritz Bauer Institut.] He was transferred to Flossenbürg camp in November 1944, where he remained until liberated on 23 April 1945 by American forces. He was proclaimed an honourary Polish citizen in 1946, and was later a key witness at the 1965 Frankfurt Auschwitz trials, in which 750 surviving Auschwitz SS personnel were tried for their acts in the camp (this trial took place under West German law, rather than international law). When cross-examined during his testimony, the defendant's lawyers tried repeatedly to suggest that Küsel, as a German criminal, likely acted as an informant for the SS, which Küsel forcefully rejected - including sarcastically asking the West German lawyer to write to Premier Cyrankiewicz if he wanted a good reference for his character. In 1981, the exiled Polish Government (descended from the pre-war Government that escaped to Britain, not the post-war Communist government) awarded him the Polish Gold Cross of Merit.
[Picture: Your Author in Berlin in 2023, the home of Otto Küsel, wearing my Otto Küsel top. The front has a Green Triangle and a 2. His story is one of extreme significance to me, who often - as a historian - wonders how I would have tried to survive the various calamities I research and write on, and wonder at my own capacity for moral courage in the face of my own mortality.] Details are limited on his post-war life, in the mid-1960s he was salesman, from his survivor pen pals it appears he married, had daughters, and had grandchildren. He died in contented comfort in Schwarzhofen, Bavaria in 1984. The stories of Prisoners 1 and 2 of Auschwitz are a stark illustration that while we are seldom masters of our circumstances, we always retain freedom in how we respond to them. As we stand here in an uncertain and difficult 21st century, we must always remember that. ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- If you like my writing, it may interest you to know my first book, about the Colonial Marines - enslaved African-Americans who joined British forces in the War of 1812, and gained their freedom - is now available for pre-order: UK Amazon: https://www.amazon.co.uk/Black-Redcoats-Colonial-Marines-1814-1816/dp/1399034014/ US Amazon: https://www.amazon.com/Black-Redcoats-Colonial-Marines-1814-1816/dp/1399034014/

item.title: V
item.dc:creator: eaglerider

item.title: Fullmetal Alchemist Tarot Deck (With quotes)
item.description:
"I'd hate to be guilty of asking too much, but I think I would be happy if I could just leave this flask..." I should point out that this is obviously a repost. I didn't create this deck. It's just been reposted so many times that I didn't think I needed to say it at this point, but a lot of comments are offering me corrections as if I made them. I appreciate the compliment, but no.
"Everything you've said will be fuel on your funeral pyre. So I think I'll begin... By burning out YOUR TONGUE."
"Because there's someone that I have to protect."
"You must move as one cohesive unit, following me, your leader. in all things and at all times. In short, we do not have the luxury of discrimination here."
"Let's suppose for a moment that I was actually a Homunculus as you say. Well now, would that be so bad?"
"The bodies are piling up all over. The Government's gonna have a hard time keeping control at this rate."
No chosen quote for this one, but instead an anime fact. While in the dub Winry's dog is designated as male, Den is actually a female in the manga.
"My incredible strength and the art of my alchemy work beautifully together! You'll find the two to be an unconquerable combination!"
"YOU DAMN IMBECILE! Do you think it's funny not to put the broom away? I ASKED YOU A QUESTION!"
"You act like you have a conscience, but you're just being sentimental. Isn't that right, Hohenheim? You think you can change things? You think one act of caring will make you human? How could it, look at your own face!"
"Sorry I couldn't keep my promise, but I'm dying first."
"Alexander! Daddy says he's going to play with us tomorrow!"
"The Sage's Stone, The Grand Elixir, The Celestial Stone, The Red Tincture, The Fifth Element; just as the Philosopher's Stone goes by many names, so it can take on many forms. It is not necessarily a Stone."
"Brother, you spoke to me of positive streams and negative streams. Because of the life I've lived, I carry both within me. I wonder which way I will flow?"
"I've felt this multiplicity before. Just how many people are inside you, Homunculus?"
"I propose a toast, my children. I shall drink to the Promised Day, and to your undying loyalty."
"The only thing worse than death is to avert your eyes from it. Look straight at the people you kill. Don't take your eyes off them for a second. And don't ever forget them, because I promise that they won't forget you."
“Stand up and walk. Keep moving forward. You've got two good legs. So get up and use them. You're strong enough to make your own path.”
"Back then, Ed was willing to sacrifice his right arm to bring my soul back. So, shouldn't the reverse be possible?"
"What do you mean 'Lower myself?' That's the only thing I've ever been. Just a simple human who couldn't save a little girl. Not even with alchemy. Who even needs alchemy, when I've got them?"
"Who am I? One name you might have for me is The World, or you might call me The Universe, or perhaps God, or perhaps The Truth. I am All, and I am One. So, of course, this also means that I am You. I am the truth of your despair, the inescapable price of your boastfulness. - ...and now, I will bestow upon you the despair you deserve. - This despair is reserved for the boastful. - You brought this outcome upon yourself. - You simply must have seen the answer with your own eyes." https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9PRSchbUMW4
"There's no such thing as a painless lesson. They just don't exist. Sacrifices are necessary. You can't gain anything without losing something first. Although if you can endure that pain, and walk away from it, you'll find that you now have a heart strong enough to overcome any obstacle. Yeah... a heart made Fullmetal."
item.dc:creator: Onlyhereforthelaughs

item.title: The Constitution prohibits federal officeholders from accepting money, payments or gifts “of any kind whatever” from foreign governments and monarchs unless they obtain “the consent of the Congress” to do so
item.description: https://www.nytimes.com/2024/01/04/us/politics/trump-hotels-foreign-business-report.html By Luke Broadwater
item.dc:creator: SisterMidnight

item.title: 'Divine Burdens', Dickens Called Them
item.description:
FP Edit: I love y'all. Seriously. You're all so smart, empathetic, and neat. It's so cool to hear stories from caregivers and folks on the spectrum alike, and it makes me feel so much less alone. I love my boy. I actually worship the little alien fey thing. He's so fascinating and sweet and brilliant and gorgeous and hilarious. I wouldn't trade him for a million neurotypical kids. I advocate like Hell for him, and he's in an incredible school with a fabulous BHT/Aide, surrounded by people who adore him. Jokes aside, I will never stop working for him to have whatever life he wants.
It's nice to have folks who understand.
item.dc:creator: hahway

item.title: Not surprised, just disappointed
item.description:
An update to this post: https://imgur.com/gallery/2AwE3qs I made a facebook post coming out as nonbinary last night, because that's the platform all my relatives would see it on. Complete radio silence from all of them except my mom. Today I asked her how Dad took it, and she said she hadn't told him yet. So I drove over under different pretenses but in reality to tell him. Apparently, between her saying that she hadn't told him and me arriving at their house, she told him. Apparently he didn't react whatsoever. I decided that since it was all out in the open, whatever his reaction (or lack thereof) was, I wasn't going to let anybody make me scared of talking about it. So I told them that I think I've alienated myself from our family. Dad's response? "Well, what kind of reaction were you expecting?" Exact quote. And that's all he said on the matter. Mom's elaborations on her feelings weren't 'I see and respect you' so much as 'I can't be bothered to care.' If your kid's in front of you saying "I think our family is going to ostracize me because of who I am," the appropriate response is not "Well, what did you expect?" That's not only a lack of support, that's voicing disapproval and blame. My relationship with him has always been pretty contentious. He wasn't a good dad. He made a lot of mistakes, which he's never apologized for. In my adulthood I've learned to forgive him anyway, but I'm still dealing with the damage he caused. Because of that, I've never really looked for his approval or respect. Which makes it surprising that this hurts so damn much. The self-destructive part of me wants to drink it away or engage in some other, worse vices, but there's another part of me that doesn't want to give him that power over me. Here I am. Nearly 27. But suddenly I'm just a scared, lonely kid again, wondering why their dad doesn't love them. Go be nice to someone for me. Love the people who love you, and forgive the people who don't. Just do me a favor and go out and be kind.
Cat tax. My idiot voidlings, Zia and Zulf.
item.dc:creator: MisterTeatime
